coredata.dc:title: A graphene-like N-annulated perylene diimide dimer compound for highly efficient electrochemiluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Xiaoli
Yang, Liuqing
Zhan, Ziying
Cieplechowicz, Edward
Chu, Kenneth
Zhang, Congyang
Jahanghiri, Sara
Welch, Gregory C.
Ding, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description: Perylene diimide (PDI) derivatives, as important optoelectronically active materials, have been explored for the development of high-performance organic light-emitting diodes, fluorescent and electrochemiluminescent (ECL) luminophores. Herein, the ECL performance and its absolute efficiency of a graphene-like N-annulated PDI derivative, tPDI2N-trimethylbenzyl (tPDI2N-TMB) in annihilation route and coreactant pathways with tri-n-propylamine (TPrA) and benzoyl peroxide (BPO) are investigated. The ECL-voltage curves with corresponding spooling ECL spectra demonstrate the light generation mechanisms. ECL emission peak wavelengths of tPDI2N-TMB at 675 nm in these systems are similar to its photoluminescence (PL) one, proving that ECL emissions from tPDI2N-TMB with TPrA or BPO coreactant system are mainly from the monomeric excited states without surface states and the dimerization processes as in the case of graphene quantum dots. The relative ECL efficiency of the tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA system was determined to be 165%, which is higher than those of many organic molecules. Considering the difference in radical behaviors between tPDI2N-TMB and Ru(bpy)3 2+, absolute ECL efficiencies are suitable to reflect the electrochemical and ECL processes objectively. The excellent ECL performance of tPDI2N-TMB reveals great potentials for the development of new PDI compounds in light emitting devices and bioanalytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-annulated perylene diimide (PDI) dimer compound
Electrochemiluminescence (ECL)
Spooling ECL spectroscopy
Absolute ECL efficiency
Relative ECL efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Relative and absolute ECL quantum efficiencies
Electrochemical and ECL studies of tPDI2N-TMB
ECL studies of tPDI2N-TMB with TPrA
ECL studies of tPDI2N-TMB with BPO
ECL efficiencies of tPDI2N-TMB with coreactants

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013–201697, DG RGPIN-2018–06556, SPG STPGP-2016–493924 (Z.D.) and DG RGPIN-2019–04392 (G.C.W.)), Canada Foundation of Innovation/Ontario Innovation Trust (CFI/OIT, 9040 (Z.D.)), Premier's Research Excellence Award (PREA, 2003 (Z.D.)), Canada Institute of Photonics Innovation (2005 (Z.D.)), Ontario Photonics Consortium (2002 Z.D.)), Canada Research Chairs Program (G.C.W.), CFI JELF (34102 (G.C.W.)), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004034 (X.Q.)), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (China) (2020JJ5226 (X.Q.)) and China Scholarship Council (CSC, 201908430010 (X.Q.)). We also thank the Electronic Shop in the Department of Chemistry and ChemBioStores at Western for their excellent service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) DPVs and (B) CV with the corresponding ECL-voltage curve for 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in 1:1 benzene/MeCN (volume ratio) solution with 0.1 M TBAPF6 as the supporting electrolyte. The CV scan rate was at 100 mV s− 1. The inset depicts the tPDI2N-TMB molecular structure. (C) The accumulated ECL spectrum of the potential pulsing experiment. (D) PL spectrum of 0.03 mM tPDI2N-TMB in 1:1 benzene:MeCN (volume ratio) solution (λ ex= 465 nm). The insets are the CIE coordinate diagram of the PL and ECL spectrum.

(A) CV with the ECL-voltage curve of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 20 mM TPrA in 1:1 benzene:MeCN (volume ratio) solution with 0.1 M TBAPF6 as the supporting electrolyte. The CV scan rate was at 100 mV s− 1. (B) The current-time response along with the corresponding ECL-time curve of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 20 mM TPrA during a potential pulsing experiment between −0.5 and 1.80 V at a frequency of 10 Hz.
Mechanisms of the coreactant ECL route with TPrA.

Spooling ECL spectra of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 20 mM TPrA in potential ranges between 0.91 and 1.31 V, between 1.31 and 1.63 V, and between 1.63 and 0.91 V, which are coded in green, red and purple, respectively. Insets are 2D viewings of the spectra following the same color codes.

(A) Cyclic voltammogram with the corresponding ECL-voltage curve of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 5 mM BPO in 1:1 benzene:MeCN (volume ratio) solution with 0.1 M TBAPF6 as the supporting electrolyte. (B) ECL mechanism of tPDI2N-TMB with BPO as coreactant. (C) Spooling ECL spectra of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 5 mM BPO. Insets are 2D viewings of the spectra. (D) The current-time with the corresponding ECL-time curves of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB with 5 mM BPO as the coreactant during a potential pulsing experiment at 10 Hz frequency.
Relative and absolute ECL efficiencies of tPDI2N-TMB with coreactants.

head.ce:title: A graphene-like N-annulated perylene diimide dimer compound for highly efficient electrochemiluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Western University, London, ON N6A 5B7, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Western University, London, ON N6A 5B7, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Chemistry and Material Science, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta T2N 1N4, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Chemistry, University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta T2N 1N4, Canada

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: X. Qin, L. Yang, Z. Zhan and E. Cieplechowicz contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perylene diimide (PDI) derivatives, as important optoelectronically active materials, have been explored for the development of high-performance organic light-emitting diodes, fluorescent and electrochemiluminescent (ECL) luminophores. Herein, the ECL performance and its absolute efficiency of a graphene-like N-annulated PDI derivative, tPDI2N-trimethylbenzyl (tPDI2N-TMB) in annihilation route and coreactant pathways with tri-n-propylamine (TPrA) and benzoyl peroxide (BPO) are investigated. The ECL-voltage curves with corresponding spooling ECL spectra demonstrate the light generation mechanisms. ECL emission peak wavelengths of tPDI2N-TMB at 675 nm in these systems are similar to its photoluminescence (PL) one, proving that ECL emissions from tPDI2N-TMB with TPrA or BPO coreactant system are mainly from the monomeric excited states without surface states and the dimerization processes as in the case of graphene quantum dots. The relative ECL efficiency of the tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA system was determined to be 165%, which is higher than those of many organic molecules. Considering the difference in radical behaviors between tPDI2N-TMB and Ru(bpy)3 2+, absolute ECL efficiencies are suitable to reflect the electrochemical and ECL processes objectively. The excellent ECL performance of tPDI2N-TMB reveals great potentials for the development of new PDI compounds in light emitting devices and bioanalytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrogenerated chemiluminescence (also called electrochemiluminescence, ECL), as a redox-induced light emission, is produced in the vicinity of a biased electrode surface by the reactions of highly active radical species electrogenerated [1–3]. The ECL processes involve two main mechanisms, annihilation and by use of a coreactant [4]. In the annihilation ECL route, the formation of a luminophore excited state will be from a homogenous electron transfer between its cation and anion radicals electroproduced [5–7]. However, in the coreactant ECL pathway, the excited state can be generated through an electron-transfer reaction of radical species of a luminophore and coreactant [8–10]. ECL has become one of the most ideal analysis tools in the fields of clinical diagnostic [11,12], imaging and biological applications due to unifying of the benefits of optical and electrochemical methods [13–15]. During the light generation processes, the ECL luminophore is a vitally important component [16]. Recently, various types of ECL luminophores have been reported, including metal complexes [17], organic dyes [18,19] and nanomaterials [20,21]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with high ECL performance have attracted great interests because of their properties of good biocompatibility, high stability and low toxicity [22,23]. Despite the various applications of GQDs to achieve, it is still quite challenging to construct their efficient ECL owing to their defects, such as low electrochemical reactivity, undesirable surface states at the edge, and easy agglomeration resulting in an aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [24]. Thus, developing novel ECL materials is essential.

Perylene diimide (PDI) derivative, as an outstanding class of optoelectronical materials along with other electronic materials, have been considered for the development of high-performance fluorescence labels, light harvesters and organic semiconducting devices because of their strong structural stability, high electron mobility, and good electron-accepting ability [25–27]. PDI derivatives can be regarded as “molecular-sized GQDs” with atomic precision due to their similar structure and are anticipated as a great alternative, Fig. S1 in Supplementary Materials (SM). Recently, our group has reported on an atomic precision graphene model compound, N-annulated PDI dimer (tPDI2N‑hex), with high ECL performance [24]. The relative ECL efficiency of the tPDI2N‑hex/tri-n-propylamine (TPrA) system was as high as 64%.

To further improve ECL performance, a N-annulated PDI dimers substituted at pyrrolic N-atom position with 2,4,6-trimethylbenzyl side-chains, tPDI2N-trimethylbenzyl (tPDI2N-TMB), was explored here, which is very close to graphene in structure (as shown in Fig. S1 and inset of Fig. 1B ). The tPDI2N-TMB molecule displays excellent ECL properties. There are some differences of the electrochemical and ECL performances between tPDI2N-TMB and tPDI2N‑hex. Because the benzyl side chains with sterically bulky alkyl substituents can lead to different intermolecular interactions as compared to alkyl side chains, with the possibility of different electronic influences on the dimeric PDI core. This work also provides a relationship study between the PDI structure and ECL property, which offers the direction for design and preparation of PDIs with desired ECL features.

tPDI2N-TMB was synthesized as we reported elsewhere [28], which is briefly described in SI. TPrA (≥98%), benzoyl peroxide (BPO, ≥98%), tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, ≥99.0%), benzene (99.8%), acetonitrile (≥99.9%) and ferrocene (Fc, 98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Canada (Mississauga, Ontario).

The electrochemical and ECL experiments were carried out using an Autolab potentiostat (PGSTAT30, Metrohm, Switzerland) with a conventional three-electrode system. The working electrode was a Pt disk electrode embedded in a soft glass tubing with a diameter of 2 mm, while the counter electrode and reference electrode were Pt wire coils. The working electrode and the ECL cell were cleaned as reported elsewhere by us [24,29]. In a N2-filled glovebox, ECL experiments were carried out in a 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB solution of 1:1 (volume ratio) benzene:acetonitrile (benzene:MeCN) with 0.1 M TBAPF6 as the supporting electrolyte. The ECL detector was a photomultiplier tuber (PMT, R928, Hamamatsu, Japan) biased at a high-voltage of −750 V, collecting the ECL signal under the flat Pyrex window at the bottom of the cell. The ECL signal in the format of photocurrent from the PMT was transformed into a voltage signal by a scale-adjustable picoammeter (Keithley 6487, Cleveland, OH). The ECL unit was in nA or μA. All potentials in this paper were calibrated to the standard hydrogen electrode one using ferrocene/ferrocenium (Fc/Fc+) as the internal reference (
E
F c + / F c
0
=0.40 V vs. SHE) [30].

The ECL spectra were measured as reported elsewhere [31] by a spectrograph with a grating of 50 l/mm blazed at 600 nm (Acton 2300i, Teledyne Princeton Instruments, MA) coupled with a charge coupled device (CCD) camera (Model DU401- BR-DD-352, Andor Technology, UK) being cooled at –65 °C. A CHI 610A electrochemical workstation (CHI 610A, CH Instruments, Austin, TX) was used to drive the electrochemical reactions during spectroscopic measurements. The unit of the ECL spectra was in counts. Before ECL measurement, a commercial mercury-argon source (HG-1, Ocean Insight, Orlando, FL) was used for wavelength calibration. Both accumulated and spooling ECL spectra were collected during potential dynamic scans.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy experiments were carried out in a 0.03 mM tPDI2N-TMB in 1:1 benzene:MeCN solution by a Fluorolog spectrophotometer (QM-7/2005, Photon Technology International, London, ON) with an excitation slit width of 0.25 mm and an emission slit width of 0.1 mm. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate diagrams were determined according to their PL/ECL spectra of tPDI2N-TMB using a custom-made program of MATLAB (R2021b), as described in SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relative and absolute ECL quantum efficiencies
ce:section.ce:para:
The absolute ECL efficiency (ΦX,A) of tPDI2N-TMB for co-reactant system is defined as the ratio of the number of photons generated to the number of charges injected [29]. Since the absolute number of photons is difficult to measure, ECL efficiency could be calculated relative to a commercialized ECL luminophore such as Ru(bpy)3(PF6)2 by taking its efficiency as 100% under the same condition using the Eq. (1) as below.
(1)
Φ
X , R
= 100 % ×
(
∫
a
b
I
E C L
d t
∫
a
b
i × d t
)
X
/
(
∫
a
b
I
E C L
d t
∫
a
b
i × d t
)
S T
where X represents the studied sample tPDI2N-TMB while ST is the standard Ru(bpy)3(PF6)2. The integrations of I ECL intensity and current versus time are the photons generated and charges injected in the system, respectively.

The absolute ECL quantum efficiency was determined by comparing the number of photons emitted with the number of electrons injected [32,33], as Eq. (2):
(2)
Φ
ECL
= 100 % ×
total
photons
total
electrons
=
100 % ×
∫
v photons
d t
v electrons
d t
= 100 % ×
∫
P C d t
(
|
i x
|
−
|
i
B G
|
)
d t
×
N A
×
k
A B S
F ×
σ
P M T
× C
where ν photons is the total ECL photon emission rate and ν electrons the total Faradaic electron rate flowing into the electrochemical cell yielding total ΦECL in a percentage of photons per electron. PC is the ECL signal as photocurrent, i x is the total current (including Faradaic and non-Faradaic current (i BG)), N A is the Avogadro's number, k ABS is the absorption correction factor, q e is a constant related to the charges per electron, F is the Faraday's constant, C is a hardware and wavelength specific factor, and σ PMT is a correction factor related to the PMT surface area, distance from PMT to electrode, and electrode reflectivity. [32,33] The detailed calculation process is illustrated in SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and ECL studies of tPDI2N-TMB
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A shows the differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in 1:1 benzene:MeCN solution within a potential range between −2.38 and 1.52 V. In the anodic scan starting from 0 V, only one reversible oxidation peak of tPDI2N-TMB was located at a formal potential of 1.30 V. However, in the cathodic scan from 0 V, tPDI2N-TMB undergoes four quasi-reversible reduction reactions to generate its radical anion at −1.40 V, dianion at −1.57 V, radical trianion at −1.86 V, and quadrivalent anion at −1.95 V, which are similar to those of the tPDI2N‑hex [24]. Fig. 1B and Fig. S3 display the ECL-voltage curves obtained simultaneously along with the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) starting from 0 V cathodically and anodically, respectively. From the CV curves of tPDI2N-TMB in the same system, the above-mentioned redox reactions are also revealed. In this annihilation pathway, tPDI2N-TMB generated ECL at both positive and negative potentials. This is because both the radical anion and cation of tPDI2N-TMB can survive long enough to meet and react, inducing the formation of an excited state, which gives off light emission upon returning to the ground state. It is plausible that the radical anions are as stable as the cations. Fig. S4 presents the potentials pulsing experiment between 1.3 V and −2.3 V, where ECL response is 1000-fold higher than that during the CV scan in Fig. 1B. In comparison with the CV-ECL experiment, the potential pulsing ECL experiment with a pulse width of 0.1 s allows more tPDI2N-TMB radical anions and cations to collide and produce excited states, resulting in higher ECL intensity.

During the potential pulsing experiments, accumulated ECL spectra of tPDI2N-TMB was acquired. As shown in Fig. 1C, the ECL peak wavelength is at 675 nm, which is slightly redshifted relative to the corresponding PL emission with a peak wavelength of 600 nm (Fig. 1D). This difference between the peak wavelengths of PL and ECL is because of the self-absorption and inner filter effect in relatively concentrated ECL solution [24]. As the sample concentration augments, the emission is absorbed more according to Beer's law, which leads to higher self-absorption inner filter effect. The CIE coordinates of tPDI2N-TMB were calculated to be (0.569, 0.419) and (0.587, 0.412) based on the PL and ECL spectra, respectively. As presented at the top right corner in the CIE diagrams, the PL and ECL with tPDI2N-TMB show both orange emission. The good agreement between PL and ECL spectra indicates that both the PL and ECL originate from the same excited state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL studies of tPDI2N-TMB with TPrA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enhance the ECL of tPDI2N-TMB, a strong reducing coreactant, TPrA (20 mM) was added, as illustrated in Fig. 2 , where the applied potential was scanned between −0.50 and 1.80 V at a scan rate of 0.1 V/s or pulsed alternatively between −0.50 and 1.80 V. Fig. 2A displays that the ECL maximum intensity (15 μA) in the ECL-voltage curve is 500 times more than that obtained from the annihilation pathway. Fig. 2B shows the ECL response of potential pulsing experiment is 2-fold more intense than that of the annihilation pathway shown in Fig. S4. It can be seen that there are two distinguished ECL maxima in the ECL-voltage curve of the tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA coreactant system. After the oxidation of TPrA (0.8 V), ECL1 (0.9 V) arose right before the oxidation of tPDI2N-TMB. Its ECL mechanism is proposed in Fig. 3A . Because TPrA is more easily oxidized than tPDI2N-TMB, the radical cation species (TPrA•+) are firstly generated. Then, TPrA•+ could remove one electron from HOMO of the tPDI2N-TMB radical anion originated from the reduction of tPDI2N-TMB by the TPrA• radical, to generate the excited state of tPDI2N-TMB as the third ECL mechanism of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPrA system discovered by Miao, Choi and Bard [34]. The ECL2 peak potential is found to be presented after the oxidation of tPDI2N-TMB. The delay of the

Onset is because the TPrA species needs some time to diffuse in the solution and react with the tPDI2N-TMB one. The two mechanisms are displayed in Fig. 3B-C. An electron transfer reaction can immediately occur between TPrA• and tPDI2N-TMB radical cations once electrogenerated in the vicinity of the electrode (Fig. 3B), which can produce the excited states. TPrA• can also reduce the neutral tPDI2N-TMB to produce a tPDI2N-TMB radical anion, which can react with the tPDI2N-TMB radical cation to generate the exited states (Fig. 3C).

Fig. 4 presents spooling ECL spectra of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 20 mM TPrA, with a potential scan cycle between −0.23 and 1.77 V, which give an insight into the ECL mechanisms [31]. Three potential ranges are color-coded in green (from 0.91 V to 1.31 V), red (from 1.31 V to 1.63 V), and purple (from 1.63 V to 0.91 V). The onset of the ECL spectrum was recorded at 0.91 V with a peak wavelength of 675 nm. At this potential TPrA is oxidized to the corresponding radical cation intermediate (TPrA•+) and part of it loses a proton to generate the neutral radical cation TPrA•, which reacts with tPDI2N-TMB to form tPDI2N-TMB•.Then, the rest TPrA•+ and tPDI2N-TMB• react to form the excited species, thereby returning to the ground state and creating ECL peak at 675 nm, as shown in Fig. 3A. ECL in this mechanism reaches a maximum and decreases till 1.31 V (Fig. 4), forming a wave like the ECL1 in the ECL-voltage curve in Fig. 2A. ECL intensity rises gain at 1.31 V and increases rapidly and finally shows the highest peak at 1.63 V, corresponding to the peak potential of the ECL-voltage curve. All ECL spectra in red in Fig. 4 illustrate a second wave ECL2 as the ECL-voltage curve in Fig. 2A. Both ECL1 and ECL2 waves are from the same excited state due to their same peak wavelength at 675 nm in the spooling ECL spectra. Upon scanning the potential in the reverse direction, the ECL intensity decreases because of the depletion in concentration of tPDI2N-TMB as well as TPrA• while the emission peak remaining at 675 nm as demonstrated by the ECL spectra in purple (Fig. 4). In comparison with our previous study of N- and S-doped GQDs [35], tPDI2N-TMB did not present either peak wavelength shifts or surface-state emission during spooling, generating by various surface traps of GQDs. Because the atomic precision structure of tPDI2N-TMB did not display any emission peak wavelength change, it is plausible that surface state, further dimerization [24] and exciplex [36,37] did not happen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL studies of tPDI2N-TMB with BPO
ce:section.ce:para: BPO as an oxidative coreactant was also added to 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB electrolyte solution in a separate experiment to explore the ECL enhancement. Fig. 5A shows the CV along with the corresponding ECL-voltage curve for 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 5 mM BPO. The maximum ECL intensity of tPDI2N-TMB with BPO is 15 μA, which is similar to that with TPrA. This demonstrates that the radical anions of tPDI2N-TMB are as stable as the radical cations, matching well with the annihilation ECL behavior in Fig. 1B. The ECL-voltage curve displays that the onset of the ECL potential of tPDI2N-TMB/BPO system is at about −1.20 V, right at the reduction of tPDI2N-TMB, and then a peak presents at about −1.40 V, which is similar to the first reduction peak potential of tPDI2N-TMB. It suggests that the tPDI2N-TMB anions give pivotal contribution for ECL emission. Fig. 5B displays the spooling ECL spectra of 0.2 mM tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 5 mM BPO as the potential was scanned from 0 V to −1.50 V and back to 0 V. The ECL variation waves are color-coded in green (forward scan) and red (backward scan). The two mechanisms are proposed as illustrated in Fig. 5C and Fig. S5. After the reduction of BPO (−0.85 V), some strongly oxidizing benzoate radicals (PhCOO•) are produced, which can oxidize the radical anion of tPDI2N-TMB produced at about −1.20 V to generate the excited states that emits ECL (Fig. 5C). PhCOO• can also oxidize the neutral tPDI2N-TMB and then generate radical cations of tPDI2N-TMB that can react with the radical anions of tPDI2N-TMB electrogenerated to emit ECL (Fig. S5). In Fig. 1C, Fig. 4 and Fig. 5B, the spectra of tPDI2N-TMB display constant shapes and peak wavelengths (675 nm) during potential scans, indicating that the excited states are independent on applied potentials and the identical excited state is generating ECL during the annihilation and coreactant processes.

With the above tPDI2N-TMB/BPO coreactant system, the applied potential was pulsed between 0 and −1.5 V, as illustrated in Fig. 5D. As a result, compared with the ECL-voltage curve during CV in Fig. 5A, the ECL intensity increases 3 times, approaching 45 μA, which is also higher than that in its annihilation pathway (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL efficiencies of tPDI2N-TMB with coreactants
ce:section.ce:para: The relative ECL efficiencies are determined as the photons emitted per redox event relative to that of Ru(bpy)3 2+, as illustrated in eq. (1). Table 1 shows that in the CV-ECL experiments, the relative ECL efficiencies of tPDI2N-TMB in the presence of 20 mM TPrA and 5 mM BPO were determined to be 46% and 16%, respectively. In fact, the relative ECL efficiency of the tPDI2N-TMB/20 mM TPrA system is lower than that of the tPDI2N‑hex/20 mM TPrA system (64%). That might be the fact that big 2,4,6-trimethylbenzyl groups of tPDI2N-TMB increase steric hindrance and slow down its diffusion or the tPDI2N-TMB radical cation has lower activity that the tPDI2N‑hex one impeding the production of excited states. In potential pulsing-ECL experiment, the relative ECL efficiencies of tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA and tPDI2N-TMB/BPO were found to be 165% and 12%, respectively, which are comparable to or even higher than those for some other reported organic molecules, as summarized elsewhere by our group [38]. The absolute ECL efficiencies (as shown in eq. (2)) of the tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA and tPDI2N-TMB/BPO coreactant systems in the CV-ECL experiments were determined to be 0.00043% and 0.00041%, respectively. And in potential pulsing-ECL experiments, the absolute ECL efficiencies in the TPrA and BPO systems were found to be 0.0014% and 0.00069%, respectively, much higher than that obtained in the CV-ECL experiments. It is evident that the absolute ECL efficiencies determined by this method do not match with the relative values, but the absolute ECL efficiencies are more consistent with the results of the experiment. The reason is that during the comparison with Ru(bpy)3 2+, the radical stability, radical reactivity and coreactant quenching on ECL are not considered, which have significant effect on the ECL efficiencies. Especially the ECL quenching effect leads to the huge unconformity in results. Although the relative ECL efficiency determination can give some insights into the relative ECL performance, the lack of standardization between relative measurements means that matching between research results is difficult or not applicable. In contrast, the absolute ECL efficiency determination by reporting the photons-out-electrons-in ratio of an ECL system can more precisely reflect the ECL processes and ECL performance, which will be more reliable than the relative ECL efficiency.

In conclusion, the electrochemistry and ECL of tPDI2N-TMB, a graphene-like N-annulated PDI compound, were investigated thoroughly. ECL from the tPDI2N-TMB was found to be robust with the similar peak wavelength as PL. Meanwhile, spooling ECL spectroscopy confirmed that ECL emissions from tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA or BPO coreactant system were mainly from the monomeric excited state without surface-state emission due to the GQD analogue tPDI2N-TMB with simple electronic structure, leading to pure luminescence and thus improved ECL efficiency. The relative and absolute ECL efficiencies of tPDI2N-TMB were also determined through annihilation and coreactant processes. The relative ECL efficiency of the tPDI2N-TMB/20 mM TPrA system is lower than that of the tPDI2N‑hex/20 mM TPrA system (64%) due to their large size and reactivity. Moreover, the ECL efficiency of the tPDI2N-TMB/TPrA system in potential pulsing-ECL experiment is as high as 165%, which shows the best ECL performances among all studied organic compound systems. As a consequence of the lack consideration of the ECL quenching, significantly underestimated or overestimated efficiencies may occur in the relative ECL efficiency determination. Therefore, the absolute ECL efficiency determination are more suitable to reflect the electrochemical and ECL processes. In addition, the ECL and electron transfer mechanisms proposed for tPDI2N-TMB provide insight into development of new PDI compounds for light emitting devices and analytical applications. In the future, hydrophilic PDI derivative might be synthesized for sensing application in aqueous solutions. Of course, tPDI2N-TMB can be coated on the electrode surface or encapsulated into the nanomaterials, such as metal organic frameworks (MOFs), for the system working in aqueous solutions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This research was funded by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013–201697, DG RGPIN-2018–06556, SPG STPGP-2016–493924 (Z.D.) and DG RGPIN-2019–04392 (G.C.W.)), Canada Foundation of Innovation/Ontario Innovation Trust (CFI/OIT, 9040 (Z.D.)), Premier's Research Excellence Award (PREA, 2003 (Z.D.)), Canada Institute of Photonics Innovation (2005 (Z.D.)), Ontario Photonics Consortium (2002 Z.D.)), Canada Research Chairs Program (G.C.W.), CFI JELF (34102 (G.C.W.)), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004034 (X.Q.)), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (China) (2020JJ5226 (X.Q.)) and China Scholarship Council (CSC, 201908430010 (X.Q.)). We also thank the Electronic Shop in the Department of Chemistry and ChemBioStores at Western for their excellent service.
X.Q. carried out data curation and analysis, validated the investigation and prepared the manuscript and revision;
Z.Z. L.Y. carried out data curation and analysis;
E.C. prepared the compound;
K.C is involved data analysis and programed MATLAB codes;
C.Z. and S.J. are involved data analysis discussion
G.W. provided advice and supervision;
Z.D. conceptualized research, designed methodology, made funding acquisition, managed project administration, wrote and finalized the manuscript and revision.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2023.142226.
